beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The cost of AI intelligence fell six thousand times in four years, and the cheap 2026 model beats the original GPT-4. The first jobs hit are drafts, summaries, scans, and reviews — work a company once paid an expensive human to do. Jevons' Paradox means cheaper AI increases attempted work: more drafts, tests, and prototypes for humans to manage. Knowledge work was priced on three hidden costs — knowing, thinking, producing — and AI attacks all three at once.

artifactLines: The cost of AI intelligence fell six thousand times in four years, and the cheap 2026 model beats the original GPT-4.
The first jobs hit are drafts, summaries, scans, and reviews — work a company once paid an expensive human to do.
Jevons' Paradox means cheaper AI increases attempted work: more drafts, tests, and prototypes for humans to manage.
Knowledge work was priced on three hidden costs — knowing, thinking, producing — and AI attacks all three at once.

beats.narration_text: The claim that AI attacks all three hidden costs of knowledge work — knowing, thinking, producing — reframed my whole job for me, and Jevons' Paradox says cheaper AI means more work attempted, not less. Help me position myself on the right side of that: (1) show me which of the three costs is hardest for AI to fully absorb and why, (2) give me a concrete example of managing more AI-attempted work rather than doing the drafting myself, and (3) name one skill that compounds as intelligence keeps getting cheaper.

props.command: The claim that AI attacks all three hidden costs of knowledge work — knowing, thinking, producing — reframed my whole job for me, and Jevons' Paradox says cheaper AI means more work attempted, not less. Help me position myself on the right side of that: (1) show me which of the three costs is hardest for AI to fully absorb and why, (2) give me a concrete example of managing more AI-attempted work rather than doing the drafting myself, and (3) name one skill that compounds as intelligence keeps getting cheaper.

beats.narration_text: Here's the number that should stop you cold. The cost of AI intelligence dropped six thousand times in four years. And the 2026 DeepSeek model that costs almost nothing is smarter than the original GPT-4. So it's not just cheaper. It's cheaper and better. Simultaneously. That's not a normal market dynamic — that's a structural collapse of what knowledge work has always been priced on.

props.artifactHeading: 6,000x cheaper. Smarter at the same time.
artifactLines: 2022 GPT-4: expensive, state of the art.
2026 DeepSeek: a fraction of the price — and smarter.
This is not a discount. It is a structural repricing of knowledge work.

beats.narration_text: The jobs hit first are the obvious ones: memos, market scans, spreadsheets, landing pages, sales emails, first drafts, summaries, slide outlines, contract reviews, twenty ideas for a campaign. A company used to need an expensive human for all of that. Now it needs a prompt. Maybe thirty seconds. Maybe twenty dollars a month. And the output is fine. Sometimes better than the junior they hired last year. The market decided.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (screenshot or chart showing junior knowledge-work job postings declining, or a concept graphic from the article)

beats.narration_text: Here's the counterintuitive part. You'd expect cheaper AI to mean less work. It actually means more work gets attempted. This is Jevons' Paradox: when something useful gets cheaper, people use more of it. More drafts, more tests, more research, more prototypes, more cold emails, more dashboards, more legal first passes. AI doesn't reduce the amount of work in the world. It increases the amount humans are expected to manage.

props.artifactHeading: Cheaper doesn't mean less. It means more.
artifactLines: When efficiency rises, total consumption rises too.
Cheaper AI → more drafts, more analysis, more everything.
You won't lose because AI can think. You'll lose because everyone around you can suddenly think 100× more.

beats.narration_text: Knowledge work was always priced around three hidden costs: the cost to know enough, the cost to think through the work, and the cost to produce the final thing. AI attacks all three. It knows enough to start. It thinks enough to draft. It produces enough to make you uncomfortable. And yes, it still makes mistakes. But cheap tools don't need to be perfect. They need to be good enough for people to use them a hundred times more.

props.artifactHeading: Three hidden costs of knowledge work
artifactLines: 1. Cost to know enough → AI starts from knowledge.
2. Cost to think through the work → AI drafts it.
3. Cost to produce the final thing → AI ships it.
Good enough × 100× more often = the new baseline.

beats.narration_text: So what actually survives? The things AI can't commoditize. Judgment about what the work should achieve. Taste about what good looks like. Accountability when the output is wrong. Trust that took years to build. Relationships that live off-screen. The ability to walk into a room and read what isn't being said. Intelligence is becoming a commodity. The bottleneck is shifting to the human layer that wraps around it.

props.artifactHeading: The human layer above the model
artifactLines: Judgment — deciding what the work should achieve.
Taste — knowing what good looks like before the output arrives.
Accountability — owning the result when it's wrong.
Trust and relationships — built over years, not tokens.

beats.narration_text: The practical prescription is this: stop competing on the output. Start competing on the input. The person who writes the best brief, who frames the right question, who catches the wrong assumption before Claude runs with it — that person is worth more every year, not less. Use AI a hundred times more than your peers. Become the one who orchestrates, not the one who produces.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B06.png (a concept graphic or diagram from the article showing the shift from producer to orchestrator / director of AI)

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Don't save this and forget it — block twenty minutes and apply one section to your actual work today. Paste this into Claude and let it map where you're still competing on output.

props.command: Here is my current job: [describe your role and daily tasks]. Identify which parts AI can already do at a fraction of my cost, which parts require human judgment, and give me a 30-day plan to shift my value toward the parts that survive.

props.title: You can't beat AI.
props.subline: Orchestrate the model, own the judgment
